genesis.literary_note: Alternates between primeval myth-form history (1–11) and detailed domestic narrative (12–50). Uses genealogy (tôlēdôt) as structural spine.
genesis.structure_note: Twelve tôlēdôt ('these are the generations of') sections divide the book. Stories are often organized in triplicates and pairs.

exodus.literary_note: Two major sections: deliverance narrative (1–18) and covenant/tabernacle materials (19–40). The Song of the Sea (15) is one of the oldest Hebrew poems.
exodus.structure_note: A chiastic structure surrounds the Decalogue at the center. The tabernacle instructions (25–31) are mirrored by their execution (35–40).

leviticus.literary_note: Almost entirely legal material, but embedded in a narrative frame (Sinai theophany). The Holiness Code (17–26) has its own literary logic: expanding circles of holiness.
leviticus.structure_note: Concentric structure: sacrifices (1–7) → priests (8–10) → cleanness (11–15) → Day of Atonement (16, center) → holiness (17–27).

numbers.literary_note: Interweaves census, law, and narrative. The book's literary structure mirrors Israel's journey: preparation → faithless first generation → wilderness → faithful second generation.
numbers.structure_note: Two censuses frame the book (ch.1 and ch.26), marking the death of the old generation and the preparation of the new.

deuteronomy.literary_note: Structured as an ANE suzerainty-vassal treaty (preamble, historical prologue, stipulations, sanctions, witnesses). The whole book is Moses's farewell address before death.
deuteronomy.structure_note: Concentric structure: historical prologue (1–4) → Decalogue (5–11) → laws (12–26) → curses/blessings (27–28) → covenant renewal (29–30) → epilogue (31–34).

joshua.literary_note: Two main sections: conquest (1–12) and distribution (13–22), with covenant renewal framing (23–24). Heavy use of repetition and fulfillment formulas ('as the LORD had commanded Moses').
joshua.structure_note: The Rahab-spies story opens the conquest; the Achan sin story interrupts it at the center. Structure highlights obedience vs. disobedience.

judges.literary_note: The famous Deuteronomic cycle: rest → apostasy → oppression → cry → deliverer → rest. Intentionally arranged to show moral deterioration. The last two stories (17–21) are appendices showing how far Israel has fallen.
judges.structure_note: The cycle repeats 7 times with increasing dysfunction. Judges ends with civil war and gang rape — the nadir of the period.

ruth.literary_note: A perfectly crafted short story with four scenes, each a dramatic encounter. The genre is closer to a novella than historical report. Hesed (covenant loyalty) is the theological theme.
ruth.structure_note: Four scenes: fields (ch.1–2) → threshing floor (ch.3, dramatic center) → city gate (ch.4a) → genealogy (ch.4b). The genealogy (surprise ending) reveals this is a Davidic origin story.

1samuel.literary_note: Samuel as transition figure; Saul's rise and fall; David's emergence. Heavy use of contrast (Eli/Hannah, Saul/David) as literary technique.
1samuel.structure_note: Three main characters in sequence: Samuel (1–7), Saul (8–15), David (16–31). Each section subverts the previous king's legitimacy in favor of the next.

2samuel.literary_note: David's reign structured as rise (1–10) → fall (11–20) → appendices (21–24). The Bathsheba/Uriah episode (11) is the turning point of the entire David narrative.
2samuel.structure_note: Concentric structure around chapter 11 (Bathsheba). Nathan's parable (12) is the center of the moral universe.

1kings.literary_note: Solomon's reign (1–11) as theological high point; division and decline (12–22). Elijah narrative (17–19, 21) as prophetic critique of royal apostasy.
1kings.structure_note: Solomon's wisdom (1–10) → apostasy (11) → division → Elijah cycle. The Elijah stories read as prophetic counterpoint to the corrupt monarchy.

2kings.literary_note: Elisha succeeds Elijah; northern kingdom falls (722 BC); southern kingdom falls (586 BC). Written retrospectively to explain why exile happened — covenant violation.
2kings.structure_note: The fall of the north (ch.17) and fall of the south (ch.25) are the two structural endpoints. Everything between is narrated under the shadow of coming exile.

1chronicles.literary_note: The Chronicler's retelling of Samuel-Kings, emphasizing David's preparations for the Temple. Nine chapters of genealogy open the book — establishing the continuity of God's people.
1chronicles.structure_note: Genealogies (1–9) → David's reign focused on Temple preparation (10–29) → Solomon's Temple construction (2 Chr 1–9).

2chronicles.literary_note: Solomon's Temple (1–9) → divided monarchy through exile (10–36). The Chronicler systematically omits material about the northern kingdom, focusing on Judah and the Temple.
2chronicles.structure_note: Ends with Cyrus's decree (36:22–23) — the same words that open Ezra, creating a literary hinge between the books.

ezra.literary_note: Return from Babylon in two waves: under Zerubbabel (1–6) and Ezra (7–10). Uses first-person Ezra memoir (7:27–9:15) embedded in third-person narrative.
ezra.structure_note: Ezra 4:8–6:18 and 7:12–26 are written in Aramaic — the diplomatic language of the Persian court — distinguishing the official records from the Hebrew narrative.

nehemiah.literary_note: Primarily Nehemiah's first-person memoirs. Wall-building narrative frames the book; Ezra's reading of Torah (ch.8) is the spiritual center.
nehemiah.structure_note: Wall rebuilt in 52 days (ch.6) → covenant renewal and reading of Torah (ch.8–10) → population and worship reforms (ch.11–13).

esther.literary_note: God's name never appears — the literary design highlights hidden divine providence. Heavy irony and reversal: the gallows Haman builds is used to hang him. Purim origin story.
esther.structure_note: Elaborate chiastic structure: two banquets → Mordecai's honor/humiliation alternating with Haman's → two more banquets. The entire book is organized around the reversal at its center.

job.literary_note: Prose frame (1–2, 42:7–17) surrounds a vast poetic dialogue. Three cycles of friend-speeches, a divine speech in the whirlwind, and an Elihu interruption. Among the most sophisticated Hebrew poetry.
job.structure_note: Prose prologue (1–2) → three dialogue cycles (3–27) → Wisdom hymn (28) → Job's final defense (29–31) → Elihu (32–37) → divine speeches (38–41) → prose epilogue (42).

psalms.literary_note: The Psalter is a five-book anthology (Books 1–5, mirroring the Pentateuch). Psalms 1 and 2 are intentional gatekeepers. The collection moves from predominantly individual lament to communal praise.
psalms.structure_note: Five books: 1–41, 42–72, 73–89, 90–106, 107–150. Each ends with a doxology. The final five psalms (146–150) are a sustained crescendo of pure praise.

proverbs.literary_note: Two major sections: wisdom poems (1–9, extended instruction speeches) and sentence collections (10–31, short proverbs). Proverbs 1:7 ('the fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom') is the thesis of the entire book.
proverbs.structure_note: Proverbs 8 (Wisdom's self-description) is the theological climax of the first section — Wisdom personified as the agent of creation.

ecclesiastes.literary_note: Qohelet ('the Preacher/Assembler') explores the limits of wisdom. The recurring הֶבֶל (hebel = vapor/vanity/breath) frames all human endeavors as fleeting. The book does not end in despair but in fear of God.
ecclesiastes.structure_note: Frame narrator introduces and concludes (1:1–11; 12:9–14) the first-person reflections. The final verdict (12:13–14) is the book's conclusion, not a contradiction of what preceded.

songofsolomon.literary_note: Hebrew erotic love poetry in the ANE tradition. Interpreted allegorically (God/Israel; Christ/Church) from ancient times but the literal reading of human love is the surface meaning. An acrostic poem of female desire and mutuality.
songofsolomon.structure_note: No clear narrative plot — a collection of poems celebrating embodied love. The woman speaks more than the man — unusual in ANE literature. The female voice is authoritative and initiating.

isaiah.literary_note: The 'fifth Gospel' — densely Christological. Four Servant Songs (42, 49, 50, 52–53). The great chiasm of chapters 1–39 (judgment) and 40–55 (comfort). Chapters 56–66 address the post-exilic restored community.
isaiah.structure_note: Isaiah 40:1–2 ('Comfort, comfort my people') is the most dramatic structural pivot in the Hebrew prophets — moving from doom to consolation.

jeremiah.literary_note: A biographical/confessional prophet — unique among the prophets for his raw personal anguish (the 'confessions' in 11–20). The New Covenant oracle (31:31–34) is Jeremiah's theological climax.
jeremiah.structure_note: Not arranged chronologically — the arrangement is thematic and literary. The LXX Jeremiah is significantly shorter and differently ordered than the Hebrew MT.

lamentations.literary_note: Five poems lamenting Jerusalem's destruction (586 BC). Four are acrostics; ch.3 is a triple acrostic (66 verses). The literary sophistication intensifies the grief — every letter of the alphabet is wrung out in mourning.
lamentations.structure_note: Chapter 3 is the center and climax — the lowest point of despair (vv.1–18) turns to the famous statement of hope ('The steadfast love of the LORD never ceases,' vv.22–23) and back to lament.

ezekiel.literary_note: Ezekiel is the most visually elaborate Hebrew prophet — the chariot-throne vision (merkabah), valley of dry bones, and final temple vision (40–48) are the three great set pieces. Extended allegories (ch.16, 23) are disturbing in their sustained intensity.
ezekiel.structure_note: Three-part structure: judgment on Israel (1–24) → oracles against nations (25–32) → restoration oracles (33–48). The pivotal ch.33 shifts from doom to hope at the fall of Jerusalem.

daniel.literary_note: Six court tales (1–6) + four apocalyptic visions (7–12). The Aramaic section (2:4b–7:28) is a literary ring structure framing the Hebrew sections. The book embodies 'resistance theology' — faithfulness under empire.
daniel.structure_note: The Aramaic ring: ch.2 (statue → 4 kingdoms) ↔ ch.7 (4 beasts → Son of Man). Ch.3 (fiery furnace) ↔ ch.6 (lions' den). Ch.4 (Nebuchadnezzar humbled) ↔ ch.5 (Belshazzar judged).

hosea.literary_note: Hosea's marriage to Gomer is the living metaphor for the entire book: God's faithful love for faithless Israel. The book alternates between judgment (because you have done this) and hope (yet I will...)
hosea.structure_note: Chapters 1–3 (Hosea's marriage and its meaning) interpret chapters 4–14 (Israel's unfaithfulness and God's response). The gospel-within-judgment structure: doom (4–10) → restoration (11–14).

joel.literary_note: A locust plague becomes the lens for the Day of the LORD. Joel 2:28–32 (the Spirit-poured-out oracle) is the most-cited OT text in Acts (Pentecost). The book moves from specific historical crisis to cosmic eschatology.
joel.structure_note: Lament over locusts (1:1–2:11) → call to repentance (2:12–17) → divine answer (2:18–27) → eschatological promise (2:28–3:21).

amos.literary_note: A shepherd-farmer prophet, not a professional. Amos's woe oracles against Israel's social injustice are among the sharpest in the prophets. The hymn fragments (4:13; 5:8–9; 9:5–6) interrupt the oracles like doxological anchors.
amos.structure_note: Oracles against nations (1–2, with Israel as the unexpected final target) → three 'hear this word' addresses (3–6) → five visions (7–9) → restoration epilogue (9:11–15).

obadiah.literary_note: The shortest OT book — one oracle against Edom for their treachery against Judah at Jerusalem's fall. The Day of the LORD applies to all nations, not just Edom.
obadiah.structure_note: Two movements: judgment on Edom (vv.1–14) → Day of the LORD for all nations and Israel's restoration (vv.15–21).

jonah.literary_note: Jonah is a satirical story about a prophet who resents God's grace toward enemies. The story mocks Jonah's exceptionalism. The fish is not the point — the book's point is Jonah's anger at God's mercy in the final chapter.
jonah.structure_note: Two parallel movements: commission-flight-storm-fish-prayer (1–2) // commission-obedience-Nineveh repents-Jonah's anger (3–4). The second movement resolves with God's question, not Jonah's answer.

micah.literary_note: Three oracles alternating judgment and hope (1–2, 3–5, 6–7). The covenant lawsuit (6:1–8) climaxes in the most concise ethical summary in the OT: 'Do justice, love kindness, walk humbly with your God.'
micah.structure_note: The Messianic oracle (5:2) — 'from Bethlehem... shall come forth for me one who is to be ruler in Israel' — is embedded in a broader judgment-restoration movement.

nahum.literary_note: A sustained poetic oracle against Nineveh (Assyria). Chapter 1 is a partial acrostic. Where Jonah showed Nineveh's repentance, Nahum announces their judgment — history has turned.
nahum.structure_note: Theological hymn on the LORD's vengeance (1:1–8) → announcement of Nineveh's fall (1:9–2:2) → vivid battle description (2:3–3:19).

habakkuk.literary_note: Unique structure: a dialogue between the prophet and God. Habakkuk complains (why do you tolerate injustice?), God answers (Babylon will judge), Habakkuk complains again (why use the unjust to judge?), God answers (the righteous live by faith). Ch.3 is a theophany psalm.
habakkuk.structure_note: Two complaint-response cycles (1:1–2:20) + psalm of resolution (3:1–19). The famous line 'the righteous shall live by his faith' (2:4) is Paul's anchor in Galatians and Romans.

zephaniah.literary_note: The Day of the LORD against Judah, the nations, and then redemption. The medieval Latin text of 1:15 ('Dies irae, dies illa') became the famous Requiem Mass text.
zephaniah.structure_note: Day of the LORD against Judah (1:1–2:3) → against nations (2:4–15) → against Jerusalem, then hope (3:1–20). Ends with one of the warmest restoration oracles in the Minor Prophets (3:14–20).

haggai.literary_note: Four precisely dated oracles urging the returned exiles to rebuild the Temple. Short, practical, theologically precise. The Temple's glory, covenant promise, and Zerubbabel's signet ring are the three theological stakes.
haggai.structure_note: Dated oracle 1 (Aug 520): rebuild the Temple → dated oracle 2 (Oct): the future glory will surpass Solomon's → dated oracle 3 (Dec): holiness and blessing → dated oracle 4 (Dec): Zerubbabel as signet ring.

zechariah.literary_note: Eight night visions (1–6) + two oracle collections (7–8, 9–14). Chapters 9–14 are among the most apocalyptic and Christological in the OT — the Triumphal Entry donkey (9:9), 30 pieces of silver (11:12–13), pierced one (12:10) are all cited in the Passion narratives.
zechariah.structure_note: The 'Deutero-Zechariah' question (chs. 9–14 as a separate collection) is a major critical debate; NT citations focus almost entirely on these later chapters.

malachi.literary_note: Structured as six disputations: God makes an assertion; the people question it ('How?'); God answers. This dialogue form is unique in the prophets. The closing chapters anticipate Elijah's return (John the Baptist) and the 'sun of righteousness.'
malachi.structure_note: Six disputation units: I love you (1:2–5) → you despise my name (1:6–2:9) → faithless to covenant (2:10–16) → weary of God (2:17–3:5) → rob God (3:6–12) → speak against God (3:13–4:3) → epilogue (4:4–6).

matthew.literary_note: Organized around five major discourses (Sermon on the Mount, Mission, Parables, Community, Eschatology), possibly mirroring the five books of Moses. Heavy use of fulfillment formula ('to fulfill what was spoken by the prophet').
matthew.structure_note: Each discourse ends with 'when Jesus had finished...' (7:28; 11:1; 13:53; 19:1; 26:1). The five-discourse structure makes Matthew the 'teaching Gospel.'

mark.literary_note: The 'immediately' Gospel — εὐθύς (immediately) drives breathless momentum. Uses intercalation (Markan sandwich) as a primary structural device. Ends with the empty tomb and silence (16:8) — the most abrupt ending in the NT.
mark.structure_note: Two halves: Jesus's ministry (1–8) → Peter's confession as pivot (8:27–30) → Jesus's journey to Jerusalem and passion (9–16). Each half opens with a boat scene.

luke.literary_note: The 'universal' Gospel — the most literary of the four. Unique material: infancy hymns (Magnificat, Benedictus, Nunc Dimittis), Good Samaritan, Prodigal Son, Zacchaeus. The longest journey section in any Gospel (9:51–19:44).
luke.structure_note: Infancy narrative (1–2) → Galilean ministry (3–9:50) → Journey to Jerusalem (9:51–19:44, 10 chapters) → Jerusalem passion (19:45–24). The journey section is Luke's unique contribution.

john.literary_note: The 'spiritual Gospel' (Clement of Alexandria). Seven signs (σημεῖα), seven I AM statements, elaborate discourses and dialogues. Written at a different level of theological reflection than the Synoptics. The prologue is among the most analyzed paragraphs in world literature.
john.structure_note: Prologue (1:1–18) → Book of Signs (1:19–12:50) → Book of Glory (13:1–20:31) → Epilogue (21). The pivot: 'the hour has come' (12:23; 13:1) marks the transition from public ministry to passion.

acts.literary_note: The second volume of Luke-Acts. Follows the geographical progression of Acts 1:8 ('Jerusalem → Judea → Samaria → ends of the earth'). Six summary statements mark the gospel's growth at structural pivots.
acts.structure_note: Jerusalem (1–7) → Judea/Samaria (8–12) → Antioch/first journey (13–14) → Jerusalem council (15, center) → second journey (16–18) → third journey (19–20) → Rome (21–28).

romans.literary_note: Paul's most sustained theological argument. Uses diatribe (imagined objector: 'What then shall we say?') as a rhetorical device. The thesis (1:16–17) is the foundation of the entire letter.
romans.structure_note: Theological argument (1–11) → ethical application (12–16). Within 1–11: condemnation (1–3) → justification (4–5) → sanctification (6–8) → Israel (9–11). Chapter 8 is the theological climax.

1corinthians.literary_note: Responds to two sources: a report from Chloe's household (1–6) and a letter from Corinth (7–16, introduced by 'now concerning'). Chapter 13 is the poetic center — a hymn to love interrupting spiritual gifts discussion.
1corinthians.structure_note: Six 'now concerning' (περὶ δέ) sections structure 7–16: marriage, food offered to idols, worship practices, spiritual gifts, collection, Apollos. Ch.15 (resurrection) is the doctrinal climax.

2corinthians.literary_note: Paul's most autobiographical letter. Three sections possibly once separate letters: reconciliation and collection (1–9), defense against 'super-apostles' (10–13). The theology of strength-in-weakness is the thematic center.
2corinthians.structure_note: The 'fool's speech' (11:1–12:13) is a sustained parody of Greco-Roman boasting rhetoric — Paul boasts about suffering and weakness, not achievement.

galatians.literary_note: Paul's most intense letter — no thanksgiving opening. Addresses the threat of 'another gospel' (1:6–9). The allegory of Hagar and Sarah (4:21–31) and the fruit of the Spirit (5:22–23) are the literary centerpieces.
galatians.structure_note: Autobiographical defense (1–2) → theological argument on justification (3–4) → ethical application (5–6).

ephesians.literary_note: Possibly a circular letter — many manuscripts lack 'in Ephesus' in 1:1. The most 'cosmic' of Paul's letters. Two halves: indicative (what God has done, 1–3) and imperative (how to live, 4–6), united by 'therefore' (4:1).
ephesians.structure_note: One extended sentence in Greek: 1:3–14 (22 verses). The Christ-hymn, the Armor of God (6:10–18), and the household code (5:22–6:9) are the literary set pieces.

philippians.literary_note: Paul's most joyful letter, written from prison. The Christ hymn (2:5–11) is the centerpiece — possibly a pre-Pauline hymn incorporated and commented on. The joy-command ('Rejoice always') appears 16 times.
philippians.structure_note: Thanksgiving + prayer (1:1–11) → Paul's situation (1:12–26) → call to unity (1:27–2:18, including Christ hymn) → travel plans (2:19–3:1) → warning against false teaching (3:1–4:1) → conclusion (4:2–23).

colossians.literary_note: The Christ hymn (1:15–20) is the most cosmological statement of Christ's supremacy in the NT. Written against a syncretistic 'philosophy' threatening the Colossian church.
colossians.structure_note: Christ hymn (1:15–20) is the theological anchor from which all arguments radiate. The 'therefore' (2:6) draws the ethical consequences of cosmic Christology.

1thessalonians.literary_note: Possibly the earliest Pauline letter. Remarkable warmth and pastoral care. The eschatological sections (4:13–5:11) address grief over the dead and readiness for the Parousia.
1thessalonians.structure_note: Thanksgiving expanded into most of the letter (1–3) — Paul's longest sustained thanksgiving. Ethical instruction (4:1–5:22) + eschatological reassurance (4:13–5:11).

2thessalonians.literary_note: Corrects a misunderstanding that the Day of the LORD has already come. The 'man of lawlessness' (2:3–12) is among the most debated apocalyptic texts in Paul.
2thessalonians.structure_note: Three sections: encouragement in persecution (1) → correction on the Day of the LORD (2) → practical instructions (3).

1timothy.literary_note: Written to Timothy in Ephesus to address false teaching and establish church order. The 'faithful sayings' (πιστὸς ὁ λόγος) appear five times in the Pastorals as embedded doctrinal summaries.
1timothy.structure_note: Church order concerns alternate with doctrinal instruction throughout. The qualifications for elders and deacons (3:1–13) are the practical center.

2timothy.literary_note: The most personal of the Pastorals — Paul's final letter before execution. The famous charge to 'rightly handle the word of truth' (2:15) and the final testimony 'I have fought the good fight' (4:7) are its emotional climaxes.
2timothy.structure_note: A farewell letter shaped by Paul's approaching death. The urgency of Timothy's calling (1:6–2:13) is rooted in Paul's own example of suffering-fidelity.

titus.literary_note: Brief and practical — church structure, qualifications for elders, ethical instruction for various groups. The 'grace that trains us' (2:11–14) and 'merciful renewal' (3:4–7) are its two doctrinal hymn-fragments.
titus.structure_note: Qualifications for elders (1) → community ethics (2) → the theological basis for those ethics (2:11–14; 3:4–7) → practical instructions (3:8–15).

philemon.literary_note: The most personal of Paul's letters — to a single individual about a runaway slave. A masterpiece of rhetoric and pastoral care. Paul uses every rhetorical device to achieve the goal (free Onesimus) without making it a command.
philemon.structure_note: A chiastic letter: Paul's thankfulness for Philemon's love (vv.4–7) → appeal for Onesimus (vv.8–20) → confidence in Philemon's love and obedience (vv.21–22).

hebrews.literary_note: Not technically a letter — probably a synagogue-style sermon ('word of exhortation,' 13:22). The most sustained OT typological argument in the NT. The 'Hall of Faith' (ch.11) is its most famous passage.
hebrews.structure_note: Alternates between doctrinal exposition and pastoral exhortation (the 'warning passages'). Structure: Christ greater than angels (1–2) → Moses (3–4) → Aaronic priesthood (5–10) → Faith and Endurance (11–13).

james.literary_note: The most 'wisdom' book in the NT — structured more like Proverbs than Paul. Directly addresses the rich vs. poor economic injustice in the early church. The 'faith without works is dead' passage (2:14–26) has been the most debated NT ethical text.
james.structure_note: No clear linear argument — thematic clusters around trials/wisdom, rich/poor, faith/works, tongue, wisdom, prayer. Tied together by the opening theme: testing produces endurance (1:2–4).

1peter.literary_note: Written to 'exiles of the dispersion' — Jewish-diaspora language applied to Gentile Christians. The 'living stone' and 'royal priesthood' language (2:4–10) are identity-forming for the community. Theology of suffering as participation in Christ.
1peter.structure_note: The household code (2:13–3:7) is embedded in a larger argument about honorable conduct among the Gentiles. The letter may be structured around baptismal catechesis.

2peter.literary_note: A farewell letter in the style of Moses's farewell (Deut 31–34) and similar Jewish testamentary literature. The scoffers-and-Flood argument (3:3–7) and the 'day of the Lord' section (3:8–13) are the theological heart.
2peter.structure_note: Virtue list and election (1) → warning against false teachers (2, borrowing from Jude) → eschatological confidence (3).

1john.literary_note: A spiral rather than linear argument — the same themes (light/darkness, love/hate, truth/lie, God's children/world) recur and deepen with each pass. Tests of authentic Christian community: moral (walking in light), social (loving one another), and doctrinal (confessing Christ in the flesh).
1john.structure_note: Three spiraling cycles (1:5–2:27; 2:28–4:6; 4:7–5:12), each addressing the same tests from a different angle. The prologue (1:1–4) mirrors John's Gospel prologue.

2john.literary_note: Brief note to 'the elect lady and her children' (a congregation). Addresses the same docetic false teachers as 1 John — commands hospitality to be withdrawn from those who deny the Incarnation.
2john.structure_note: Greeting (1–3) → truth-and-love command (4–6) → warning against deceivers (7–11) → closing (12–13).

3john.literary_note: The most personal NT letter — to Gaius, a church leader. The tension between Gaius (hospitable) and Diotrephes (power-hungry, inhospitable) is a window into church conflict in the apostolic period.
3john.structure_note: Gaius commended (1–8) → Diotrephes rebuked (9–10) → Demetrius commended (11–12) → closing (13–15).

jude.literary_note: A vivid and compressed letter using three OT types (Israel in wilderness, angels, Sodom) and three OT characters (Cain, Balaam, Korah) to characterize the false teachers. Quotes the Book of Enoch (v.14) — an apocalyptic Jewish text.
jude.structure_note: Appeal to contend for the faith (1–4) → three typological judgments (5–7) → character of the intruders (8–16) → instruction for the faithful (17–23) → doxology (24–25).

revelation.literary_note: A complex literary work in three genres: letter (1–3), apocalyptic vision (4–22), prophecy throughout. The most symbolically dense book in the NT, drawing from Daniel, Ezekiel, Zechariah, Isaiah. Must be read with OT background in mind.
revelation.structure_note: Seven letters (2–3) → throne room (4–5) → seven seals → seven trumpets → interlude (10–11) → seven signs → seven bowls (15–16) → Babylon's fall (17–18) → new creation (19–22). Recapitulation theory: the cycles repeat the same events, not sequential.
